attributes.stem: A previously well 25-year-old woman presents with a one week history of right eye reduced vision and pain with eye movements. She has also recently noticed tingling in her fingers and this is worse on hot days. She has no medical history. On examination her visual acuity in the left eye is 6/6 and 6/60 in the right. The right pupil is 4 mm diameter and minimally reactive, whilst the left is 4 mm diameter and reactive. There is an relative afferent pupillary defect in the right eye. Her red saturation is 50% in the right eye compared to 100% in the left eye. She has pain with all extra-ocular movements in the right eye. Fundoscopy shows a mildly swollen right optic disc and a pink left disc.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following investigations would be the most appropriate?
attributes.explanation:
This is a classical presentation of optic neuritis - occurring in young females presenting with reduced vision and pain with eye movement. Uhthoff's Sign is described with neurological symptoms [tingling in fingers] worsened by an increase in body temperature - this occurring in the setting of demyelinating disorder such as multiple sclerosis. Key examination findings are pain with eye movement and evidence of optic nerve dysfunction [RAPD, decreased red saturation and a swollen optic disc].

An MRI brain and orbits with gadolinium and fat suppression would be diagnostic demonstrating inflammation of the optic nerve in addition to brain demyelinating lesions suggestive of multiple sclerosis. If a demyelinating lesion is present, pulsed intravenous therapy is indicated. Absence of findings suggestive of a demyelinating disorder would assist in determining the need for further investigation including lumbar puncture, ESR, ACE, syphilis serology, chest X-ray or CT.

MRI is the most appropriate investigation given the role in diagnosis, guiding treatment and determining need for further investigation in the setting of optic neuritis. Lumbar puncture would not be the most appropriate investigation as it provides less diagnostic information compared to MRI; however presence of IgG oligoclonal bands in the cerebrospinal fluid in conjunction with MRI findings would provide evidence towards a diagnosis of multiple sclerosis.

CT chest, visual field testing and CRP are not considered first line investigations and would serve as second line or ancillary investigations. CT chest has utility in further investigation of demyelinating disorders in the absence of evidence of multiple sclerosis. Visual field testing is normally performed as an adjunct investigation in the setting of optic neuritis to determine the degree of field defect secondary to optic nerve dysfunction. Finally, CRP would not provide specific information regarding diagnosis or influence initial management.
learningPoints: An MRI scan is the most appropriate investigation for suspected brain demyelinating lesions.
references: Ehlers JP, Shah CP. The Wills eye manual: Office and emergency room diagnosis and treatment of eye disease. Philadelphia: Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2016
